summary: Gruber reviews the 2021 M1 iPad Pros by framing them as a historic milestone in Apple's silicon journey. He traces how Apple's A-series chips went from being far slower than Intel Macs to surpassing them, and how the M1 chip now creates perfect equilibrium between iPad and Mac performance. The XDR display on the 12.9-inch model is singled out as the best portable display ever made. Center Stage and the white Magic Keyboard get positive but measured treatment. However, Gruber argues the elephant in the room is iPadOS itself — the software is now what's holding back the hardware, inverting the old Intel-era problem where hardware held back the Mac.
key_points: The M1 iPad Pro marks the moment Apple achieved performance equilibrium between iPads and Macs — the same chip in both product lines
The XDR display on the 12.9-inch model is, pound for pound and dollar for dollar, the best computing display Gruber has ever used
Apple's A-series chips surpassed Intel laptop chips years ago, but some people went into denial about it
iPadOS is now the bottleneck holding back iPad Pro hardware, inverting the old dynamic where Intel chips held back Macs
The white Magic Keyboard looks great but will likely show wear and staining over time
money_quotes: With the new M1 iPad Pros, Apple has achieved equilibrium. It's literally the exact same chip.
The iPad Pro is a better machine than the MacBook Pro because it has a far better display.
This iPad Pro display is the single best display, desktop or portable, I've ever used.
In the same way that Intel's chips were holding back Macs, iPadOS has been holding back iPad Pros.
This hardware is indisputably amazing, and iPadOS is fine for casual use. But it still feels like I'm trying to do fine detail work while wearing oven mitts for my day-to-day work.

Some folks went into denial when that happened, arguing that it wasn't so, that benchmarks couldn't tell the whole story when Apple's chips were only running a 'phone OS' and Intel's chips were running 'real OSes'.
I was wrong about the mouse pointer thing — Apple brought that to iPads last year, in splendid fashion. But the x86 thing? Nailed it.
key_insight: The M1 iPad Pro represents the moment Apple's silicon ambitions reached their logical conclusion — hardware parity between iPad and Mac — but iPadOS is now the constraint preventing iPad Pro from fulfilling its potential.
